coredata.dc:title: Fluorescence detections of hydrogen peroxide and glucose with polyethyleneimine-capped silver nanoclusters
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Zhou, Ting
Su, Zhu
Wang, Xinyi
Luo, Minchuan
Tu, Yifeng
Yan, Jilin
coredata.dc:description: Detection of hydrogen peroxide is of significant importance for biological assays, and fluorescence methods are intensively reported for this purpose. Due to the highly oxidative property of this species, usually fluorescence quenching is obtained during the interactions and decreased signals are rendered. In this report, this oxidative property was adopted for an increased fluorescence signaling. Photoluminescent silver nanoclusters (AgNCs) were synthesized with polyethyleneimine as the stabilizer. This fluorescence from these nanoclusters could be quenched by reduced glutathione (GSH) through an interaction from its thiol group. As an oxidant, hydrogen peroxide converted GSH into an oxidized form (GSSG) with an elimination of the free thiols, and inhibited the quenching. This interaction presented an increased response toward hydrogen peroxide in the range of 0.1–20 μM with a detection limit of 35 nM. The scheme was further coupled with glucose oxidase for a glucose analysis down to 0.11 μM. This method was selective and was successfully applied for glucose measurement in human serum samples.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Silver nanoclusters
Glutathione
Fluorescence
Oxidation
Hydrogen peroxide
Glucose

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of the polyethyleneimine-capped silver nanoclusters (PEI-AgNCs)
PEI-capped silver nanoclusters and their interactions hydrogen peroxide
Analytical performances toward hydrogen peroxide and glucose

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Fluorescence excitation and emission spectra of the AgNCs. The spectra were obtained with 450 nm of emission and 375 nm of excitation respectively.

Fluorescence spectra of the silver nanoclusters (a), silver nanoclusters with 70 μM GSH (b) and 70 μM GSH together with 50 μM H2O2 (c). GSH with or without H2O2 were first incubated in 0.1 M Tris for 60 min. Then the mixtures were applied to AgNCs for 20 min incubation. The fluorescence signals were obtained with 375 nm excitation.

(A) Fluorescence responses to H2O2 samples at different concentrations (from a to k: 0, 0.1, 0.5, 0.8, 1, 2, 4, 5, 8, 15, 20 μM H2O2). (B) The linear plot of the fluorescence recovery. H2O2 samples were first incubated with 80 μM GSH under 0.1 M Tris (pH = 9.7) for 60 min, and then the mixtures were applied onto AgNCs for 10 min incubation. The fluorescence responses were collected with 375 nm excitation.

(A) Fluorescence responses to glucose samples at different concentrations (from a to l: 0, 0.8, 1, 4, 5, 6, 8, 10, 20, 30, 40, 50 μM glucose). (B) The linear plot of the fluorescence recovery. The experiments were performed with 50 U/mL GOx and all other experimental conditions were the same as in Fig. 3.

Fluorescence recoveries from different species. The concentration of glucose was 20 μM, while that of Cl−, Na+, K+, HPO4 2− were 2 mM, fructose, lactose, glycine (gly), tryptophan (try), histidine (his), uric acid (UA) were 400 μM, maltose, NO3 − were 200 μM, Mg2+, Fe3+ were 100 μM, Ca2+ was 20 μM, S2O8 2− was 50 μM, ascorbic acid (AA) was 30 μM, lactic acid (LA) was 1.5 mM, choline was 40 μM. All other experimental conditions were the same as in Fig. 3.
Working principle of the hydrogen peroxide and glucose detection.
Analytical performances of different glucose detection schemes.

head.ce:title: Fluorescence detections of hydrogen peroxide and glucose with polyethyleneimine-capped silver nanoclusters

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: The Key Lab of Health Chemistry and Molecular Diagnosis of Suzhou, College of Chemistry, Chemical Engineering and Materials Science, Soochow University, 199 Ren'ai Road, Industrial Park, Suzhou 215123, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: The Key Lab of Health Chemistry and Molecular Diagnosis of Suzhou
College of Chemistry, Chemical Engineering and Materials Science

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: The Key Lab of Health Chemistry and Molecular Diagnosis of Suzhou, College of Chemistry, Chemical Engineering and Materials Science, Soochow University, 199 Ren'’ai Road, Industrial Park, Suzhou, China 215123

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Detection of hydrogen peroxide is of significant importance for biological assays, and fluorescence methods are intensively reported for this purpose. Due to the highly oxidative property of this species, usually fluorescence quenching is obtained during the interactions and decreased signals are rendered. In this report, this oxidative property was adopted for an increased fluorescence signaling. Photoluminescent silver nanoclusters (AgNCs) were synthesized with polyethyleneimine as the stabilizer. This fluorescence from these nanoclusters could be quenched by reduced glutathione (GSH) through an interaction from its thiol group. As an oxidant, hydrogen peroxide converted GSH into an oxidized form (GSSG) with an elimination of the free thiols, and inhibited the quenching. This interaction presented an increased response toward hydrogen peroxide in the range of 0.1–20 μM with a detection limit of 35 nM. The scheme was further coupled with glucose oxidase for a glucose analysis down to 0.11 μM. This method was selective and was successfully applied for glucose measurement in human serum samples.

ce:section.ce:para: Reactive oxygen species (ROSs) are a series of intermediate products of molecular oxygen produced in living organisms during reduction processes [1]. Hydrogen peroxide (H2O2), as an important ROS, plays a significant role in various physiological processes [2]. It functions as an oxidative stress maker [3], a signaling molecule in cells [4], and is a vital defense against pathogen invasions [5]. Excessive H2O2 damages the cells, resulting in some serious diseases, such as diabetes, Alzheimer's disease, cardiovascular disorders, etc. [6]. The H2O2 level in living human bodies is critical for clinical diagnosis [7]. Also, because H2O2 is the product of some enzymatic oxidations, its measurement can be extended onto a wide range of analytes. One example is the analysis of glucose [8]. Under the catalysis from glucose oxidase, glucose is oxidized with hydrogen peroxide as a co-product. Because of the strong linkage between diabetes and blood glucose level and the large population suffered from this disease, it is of great importance to monitor glucose and thus hydrogen peroxide in biological samples [9,10]. Various analytical techniques have been employed for these purposes, including fluorescence [11], electrochemistry [12], spectrophotometry [13] and chemiluminescence [14]. In these methods, fluorescence detections are advantageous for their high sensitivity. One popular strategy is based on the phenomenon that hydrogen peroxide could lead to oxidative bleaching of some fluorophores and quenching of the fluorescence [15]. Due to the high background from the blank samples, this off signaling scheme (signal decreases with the amount of analyte) usually suffers from instability of signal and lower reproducibility.

Silver nanoclusters (AgNCs) have been reported for the purpose of fluorescent measurements [16,17]. These NCs come with metallic cores comprising limited number (a few to tens) of silver atoms [18]. Due to their ultra-small sizes approaching the Fermi wavelength of the electrons, some of these AgNCs are highly luminescent and can be used as fluorescent probes. Targets, such as copper ions [19], biothiols [20] and proteins [21] have been quantified through the change in fluorescence intensity of the AgNCs. In most of these analyses, the target species cause decreases of the nanocluster fluorescence and thus off signals are provided.

In this report, an alternate strategy was developed for hydrogen peroxide. First AgNCs with blue fluorescence emission were synthesized through a simple one-pot procedure. This fluorescence was significantly quenched by a thiol containing species, reduced glutathione (GSH). The introduction of hydrogen peroxide inhibited the quenching through an oxidation process and an increased signaling was presented. A further coupling to an enzymatic catalysis provided a means for sensitive measurement of glucose.

Polyethyleneimine (PEI) (MW = 10,000) was obtained from Meryer Chemical Technology Co. (Shanghai, China). Silver nitrate (AgNO3), formaldehyde (HCHO) and hydrogen peroxide (H2O2, 30 wt%) were from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co. (Shanghai, China). Glutathione (reduced) (GSH) was from Aladdin Chemical Reagent Co. (Shanghai, China). Oxidized glutathione (GSSG), glucose oxidase (GOx), glucose and two human serum samples (mixed) were from Yuanye Biotechnology Co. (Shanghai, China). Choline chloride and L-lactic acid (LA) were from Shanghai Macklin Biochemical Co. Ltd (Shanghai, China). Dialysis tubing with molecular weight cutoff (MWCO) as 3500 was from Solarbio Technology Co. (Beijing, China). All other chemicals were of analytical grade and used as received. Double-distilled water was used throughout the whole procedure.

Fluorescence signals were collected on an F-2500 spectrofluorometer (Hitachi, Japan) equipped with a xenon lamp excitation. This instrument was configured with both excitation and emission widths at 10 nm, and a bias voltage of 700 V was applied onto the photomultiplier. Ultraviolet-visible absorption spectra were measured with a TU-1900 UV–vis spectrophotometer (PGeneral Instruments, China). Microscopic images of the nanoclusters were obtained on a Tecnai G220 transmission electron microscope (TEM, 200 kV) (FEI, USA). A OneTouch UltraEasy glucose meter from Johnson and Johnson Medical Ltd (China) was applied as a reference for glucose measurement.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of the polyethyleneimine-capped silver nanoclusters (PEI-AgNCs)
ce:section.ce:para: The silver nanoclusters were synthesized with a one-pot method as reported in the previous literature [22]. All glassware used was soaked in chromic acid overnight and then thoroughly rinsed with water. In a 50-mL flask, 1 mL 94 g L−1 PEI and 0.7 mL water were mixed under magnetic stirring. Then 2.5 mL 0.1 M AgNO3 was added and stirred for 2 min. After that, 0.8 mL 1 M formaldehyde was introduced and stirred for another 2 min. The final product was kept at room temperature in dark for 4 days and then stored at 4 °C in a refrigerator before further use.

All detections were conducted under ambient conditions unless stated otherwise. The stock PEI-AgNCs were first diluted 50-fold with water. For the detection of H2O2, 100 μL H2O2 samples at different concentrations were mixed with 650 μL Tris buffer (0.1 M). Then 80 μL GSH (1.0 mM) and water were added to give a final volume of 1.0 mL. After 60-min incubation, 100 μL of the diluted PEI-AgNCs was added to the mixture solution. With a 10-min reaction, the fluorescence spectra of the mixtures were collected on the spectrofluorometer.

For glucose detections, 50 μL glucose samples at different concentrations were incubated with 100 μL, 50 U/mL GOx at 37 °C for 15 min. Then, 650 μL Tris buffer (0.1 M) and 80 μL GSH (1.0 mM) were added into the mixtures, and the mixtures were diluted to 1.0 mL with water. All remaining steps were the same as those for standard hydrogen peroxide.
Serum samples were diluted 30-fold with water, and then 100 μL aliquots were taken and analyzed by the steps described above for glucose measurement.

ce:section.ce:section-title: PEI-capped silver nanoclusters and their interactions hydrogen peroxide
ce:section.ce:para: Silver nanoclusters capped by PEI were obtained in aqueous solution through a one-pot reduction [23]. In this process, PEI functioned as the stabilizer and formaldehyde as the reducing agent. As compared with thiolated ligands, the branched polymer provided polyvalent interactions between the metal core and the ligand [23,24]. The TEM image shows the obtained AgNCs have an average diameter of about 2 nm (Fig. S1). This ultra-small size made the clusters into molecular species, and transition of the transitions of free electrons of the Ag nanoclusters brought in size-dependent optical properties [24,25]. These AgNCs gave a yellow color under sunlight and emitted blue fluorescence under a 365-nm UV-lamp. The UV–vis absorption spectrum shows that the AgNCs absorb strongly at 269 nm and 348 nm (Fig. S2), and the fluorescence spectrum displays a maximum emission at 450 nm under excitation at 375 nm (Fig. 1 ). This fluorescence increased after the synthesis, and no decay was observed during the following two week (Fig. S3). This stability might be attributed to the multiple binding from the stabilizer PEI [26–28].

Because of its highly oxidative property, hydrogen peroxide was found an efficient quencher toward a lot of fluorophores, and this effect has been intensively adopted for the constructions of “off” detection schemes [6,7,15,16]. Since the relative high background signal, these decreasing signals usually suffered from narrow linear range and high detection limit. In a similar way, the scheme was tested with the obtained AgNCs as well. The introduction of a relative high concentration of hydrogen peroxide (100 μM) only gave a minor change of the signal (4.51%, as shown in Fig. S4). It was first supposed that this stability might come from the reductive environment from the preparation step (with remaining formaldehyde), but with a removal of the small molecules from the AgNCs through dialyses (details provided in the supplementary materials), the same result was obtained. This stability over hydrogen peroxide might be attributed to the relative alkaline condition adopted (around pH 9): as the involvement of protons in the reaction (1), the high pH value would significantly lower the oxidative capability. This result made it difficult to take the AgNCs as an immediate probe for hydrogen peroxide, and an alternative strategy was required.
(1)
H 2
O 2
+
2H +
+ 2e →
2H 2
O

From the synthetic process, PEI provided a suitable accommodation for the silver nanoclusters [24]. Previous studies showed that strong interaction between thiol group and the silver atoms would break this linkage [23,24]. Glutathione is a short peptide containing cysteine. The lone electron pairs of the cysteine thiol are capable of entering these vacant d orbitals of the silver atoms, with a formation of AgS bond (bond energy as 216.7 ± 14.6 kJ mol−1). This promotes the aggregation of the clusters by chemisorption on the surface of the nanoclusters, thereby results in larger nanoparticles. Since the fluorescence of the nanoclusters is highly dependent on their sizes, this aggregating into large-sized nanoparticles will significantly change the luminescence property [18]. After GSH was introduced to the AgNCs, the fluorescence intensity decreased, and this decrease was related to the concentration of GSH (Fig. S5). The UV–vis absorption spectra reveal an obvious red-shift of the peak to around 450 nm from the aggregation (Fig. S6) [29].

The free thiol terminal in GSH renders a reductive property. In mammalian cells, GSH functions as an important antioxidant and plays a crucial role in physiological function, such as eliminating ROSs [30,31]. GSH interacts with the oxidative species, and as a result, itself is converted into an oxidized form (GSSG). This reaction removes the free thiols. When applied onto the AgNCs, oxidative species can prevent them from the aggregation. This kind of interaction is estimated to provide an increased fluorescence response toward the oxidative species, such as hydrogen peroxide (Scheme 1 ).

To test the feasibility of the scheme, AgNCs samples were mixed with different components. Fig. 2 shows that as compared with the quenched signal from interaction with GSH (70 μM) (Fig. 2b), the additional hydrogen peroxide introduced onto the AgNCs rendered a much higher response (Fig. 2c). In this process, H2O2 oxidized the reduced glutathione (GSH) into the oxidized form (GSSG):
(2)
2GSH +
H 2
O 2
→ GSSG +
2H 2
O

The large steric hindrance from the sulfur atoms in the GSSG blocked the disulfur group from interacting with the NCs and being adsorbed onto their surface (Fig. S7) [31]. As a result, the AgNCs stayed in their original dispersed state without formation of large nanoparticles. This recovery of the fluorescence provided a means for the measurement of hydrogen peroxide.

To obtain the best signals for the H2O2 measurement, various experimental parameters, such as the pH value, the reaction time and the concentration of GSH were optimized. We first evaluated the influence of the pH value on the fluorescence signaling. Only alkaline conditions were considered since the AgNC fluorescence intensity descended dramatically with the ascending acidity. The extent of the recovery toward the fluorescence intensity (F/F0, where F and F0 were the fluorescence intensities with and without H2O2 respectively) increased with the pH values (Fig. S8), and pH 9.7 (Tris only) was adopted. The influence of the incubation between H2O2 and GSH was also investigated. As a reasonable result of a higher completeness of converting GSH into GSSG, longer incubations were favorable for a greater recovery extent (Fig. S9). On the other aspect, long reactions would lower the practicality and effectiveness of the analytical method. With both factors taken into consideration, 60 min was selected for the followed detections. Furthermore, the interaction between H2O2-GSH and AgNC was also evaluated. The results showed that the fluorescence intensity decreased rapidly within the first a few minutes and remained unchanged thereafter, and 10 min was selected (Fig. S10). The GSH concentration was also evaluated and 80 μM was found suitable and adopted (Fig. S11). In a similar way, different amount of the AgNCs was tested and 100 μL was adopted to give a good balance between signal recovery and reproducibility (Fig. S12).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analytical performances toward hydrogen peroxide and glucose
ce:section.ce:para: Under the optimized conditions, hydrogen peroxide samples at different concentrations were measured. Fig. 3 shows that the F/F0 value linearly relates to the concentration of hydrogen peroxide in the range of 0.1–20 μM (with a correlation coefficient R2 = 0.9949), and a limit of detection (LOD) of is calculated to be 35 nM (as three times the standard deviation of the background noise, 3σ). In the repeatability test, seven repeated measurements of 0.1 μM hydrogen peroxide solution gave a relative standard deviation (RSD) of 1.05%, indicating a good reproducibility of this detection.

To examine the specificity of this scheme, some potential interfering substances in human serum were analyzed along with glucose. As shown in Fig. 5 , only glucose rendered an obvious response, whereas these interferences gave signals close to that of the blank. This good selectivity would be helpful in practical applications.

To verify the applicability of this method, human serum samples were analyzed. Because of the high concentration of blood glucose (millimolar level) [9], a large-fold dilution (300-fold as total) was directly adopted without further pretreatment. This reduced the influences from possible interferences and was made possible by the high sensitivity of the scheme. The concentration of the serum glucose was found in the normal range (Table 2 ). Standard additions of a glucose standard were performed and satisfactory recoveries were obtained. For further validation the reliability of the above results, these serum samples were also measured with a commercially available glucometer. The values from our detection were in good accordance with those from the glucometer, indicating good reliability and potential applicability of the scheme.

In summary, we here established a method for the determination of H2O2 based on the AgNCs-GSH system. Unlike conventional quenching scheme, hydrogen peroxide counteracted the effect from GSH and increased responses were provided. The coupling with glucose oxidase was successfully demonstrated for enzymatic reaction. Hence, this detection scheme provides can be further extended for other analysis with related oxidases (e.g. with oxidase for uric acid analysis, choline oxidase for choline, lactate oxidase for lactic acid, etc.).

Ting Zhou: Methodology, investigation and revision; Zhu Su: Methodology, investigation and original draft; Xinyi Wang: Methodology, investigation; Minchuan Luo: Methodology, investigation; Yifeng Tu: Conceptualization; Jilin Yan: Conceptualization, writing - reviewing and editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21305100), the Project of Scientific and Technologic Infrastructure of Suzhou (SZS201708) and the Priority Academic Program Development of Jiangsu Higher Education Institutions (PAPD).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.saa.2020.118881.
